concept.title: How validation works
concept.shortdesc: markdown-validator processes your file in three stages: it parses the front matter and document body, evaluates each rule in the rule set against your file, and produces a scored report that identifies every rule your file passes or fails.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Understand the three stages of markdown-validator: parsing your file, evaluating rules, and producing a scored report.

image.alt: Flowchart showing Stage 1 Parse (extract YAML front matter and render body to HTML), Stage 2 Evaluate (apply header rules, body rules, and workflows), and Stage 3 Report (score, pass/fail per rule, mitigation messages), leading to an exit-0 merge or exit-1 blocked outcome.

dd.p: The tool reads your .md file and splits it into two parts:

ul.li: Front matter. The YAML block delimited by --- at the top of the file. The tool extracts all key-value pairs from this block.
Document body. The Markdown content below the front matter. The tool renders this to HTML so it can be queried structurally.

dd.note: Your Markdown file must begin with a front-matter block. Files without a --- opening block produce a parse error and cannot be validated. Follow Resolve a missing front-matter block if this happens to you.

dd.p: The tool applies each rule in the rule set to your file, one at a time. Each rule either passes or fails. Rules are independent; a failing rule does not stop evaluation of the remaining rules.

Some rules are grouped into workflows — sequences that apply conditional logic. For example, a workflow might check heading structure only when a specific metadata value is present. As an author, you do not need to understand how workflows are written; the report shows you their outcome just as it shows individual rule results.
The tool produces a report that shows:

ul.li: A score — the number of rules that passed out of the total number evaluated (for example, 24/26)
A pass/fail status for each rule
A mitigation message for each failed rule, explaining what to fix

section.title: What the score means
section.p: The score is rules passed / total rules. Not all failures block your pull request — each rule is classified as Required (blocks CI, exit code 1) or Suggested (informational only, exit code 0). See Validation levels.

section.title: Example output
section.codeblock:
Validating: docs/tutorial-deploy-app.md Rule set: rules/tutorial.json
✓ [1] topic-must-be-tutorial ✓ [2] must-have-h1 ✓ [3] h1-must-start-with-tutorial ✗ [4] description-must-exist → Add a description field to the front matter. ✓ [5] ms-date-must-be-fresh ✗ [6] title-under-60-chars → Title must be under 60 characters.
Score: 4/6 FAILED
section.p: ✓ = passed, ✗ = failed, → = mitigation message (what to fix).
